categories.name: Web Architecture
categories.description: Core strategies and patterns for building and delivering web applications.

topics.name: Rendering Fundamentals
topics.description: The basic process of converting code and data into viewable HTML.

topics.name: Static and Server Rendering
topics.description: Strategies involving pre-built files and server-side generation.

topics.name: Performance and Hydration
topics.description: Optimizing user experience through efficient JavaScript execution and loading.

questions.question_text: What was the primary reason web development moved from static websites to Multi-Page Applications (MPAs)?
questions.explanation: Multi-Page Applications were developed because websites needed to be more dynamic, allowing the appearance to change whenever the underlying data changes on the server.
questions.hint: Think about the need for dynamic data.

choices.text: To eliminate the need for server-side code.
To allow the website to change dynamically when underlying data changes.
To provide an app-like experience with zero page reloads.
To make websites compatible with the first iPhone.

questions.question_text: In the Single Page Application (SPA) paradigm, how is the user interface typically rendered after the initial shell is loaded?
questions.explanation: An SPA starts with one HTML page as a shell, then executes JavaScript in the browser to render the UI and fetch data with additional HTTP requests.
questions.hint: What executes in the browser to build the page?
questions.answer_text: By executing JavaScript in the browser to render the UI and fetching data via additional HTTP requests.

questions.question_text: What defines the 'Best of Both Worlds' approach in modern Server-Side Rendering (SSR)?
questions.explanation: Modern SSR renders the initial page load dynamically on the server for speed and SEO, then 'hydrates' to client-side JavaScript to provide a smooth SPA experience.
questions.hint: It combines server-side initial load with client-side interactivity.

choices.text: Rendering everything in the browser first, then backing it up to a server.
Using only static HTML with no JavaScript allowed.
Server-side rendering for the initial load, followed by client-side hydration for an app-like experience.
Generating thousands of static files and never using a server again.

questions.question_text: What user experience issue can occur during the 'hydration' process on a large website?
questions.explanation: During hydration, the app might feel 'frozen' because the JavaScript is busy executing to take over the rendering process, making the page non-interactive for a short time.
questions.hint: Think about the app's responsiveness while JavaScript is loading.

choices.text: The page colors might revert to black and white.
The website might feel frozen while JavaScript executes to take over the rendering.
Search engines will be unable to find the homepage.
The server will crash due to too many requests.

questions.question_text: How does Streaming SSR improve performance compared to traditional SSR?
questions.explanation: Streaming SSR allows the server to render and send content concurrently in multiple chunks rather than waiting to send everything all at once, making the UI interactive faster.
questions.hint: It involves breaking the response into chunks.
questions.answer_text: It renders server-side content concurrently in multiple chunks instead of all at once, allowing the UI to become interactive faster.

learning_cards.title: Resumability Mechanism
learning_cards.front: How does 'Resumability' in the Quick framework avoid the hydration problem?
learning_cards.back: It serializes the website, data, and event listeners directly into the HTML. JavaScript is broken into tiny chunks that are lazy-loaded only when needed, meaning no hydration phase is required.
